draft.introduction: The Rule of St. Benedict (c. 530 CE) is the foundational document of Western monasticism. Its Prologue summons the reader to “listen with the ear of the heart” and establishes a school of the Lord’s service ordered by prayer, work, and obedience.

translation.text: Listen, my son, to the master’s instructions, and attend to them with the ear of your heart. This is advice from a father who loves you: welcome it and faithfully put it into practice. The labor of obedience will bring you back to him from whom you had drifted through the sloth of disobedience. We intend to establish a school for the Lord’s service.
translation.original_text: Obsculta, o fili, praecepta magistri, et inclina aurem cordis tui

translator_notes.note: The phrase aurem cordis (ear of the heart) establishes Benedict’s entire program: monastic life is fundamentally about listening—to Scripture, to the abbot, to the brothers, to silence itself. The Rule’s moderation (compared to Eastern asceticism) made it the standard for Western religious life for fifteen centuries.
